channel.title: Posts on EnricoBDev's Blog
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on EnricoBDev's Blog

item.title: How to handle exceptions in Java
item.description:
Exceptions in Java

What could possibly go wrong?

In the lifetime of an application not everything will go as planned, mistakes will be made both from the user and the programmer but sometimes you will just be unlucky (a random bit flip caused by a cosmic ray will change your game state… and give a speedrunner a huge timesave)

We can categorize the sources of error in two groups:

  • internal errors, for example the user wants to do a division by zero or a method is called on a null reference
  • external errors, for example a comunication error with a remote DBMS or even an error inside the JVM, for example an “Out of memory error”

The OOP way of handling those problems is by handling them outside the normal control flow.
item.content:
Exceptions in Java

What could possibly go wrong?

In the lifetime of an application not everything will go as planned, mistakes will be made both from the user and the programmer but sometimes you will just be unlucky (a random bit flip caused by a cosmic ray will change your game state… and give a speedrunner a huge timesave)

We can categorize the sources of error in two groups:

  • internal errors, for example the user wants to do a division by zero or a method is called on a null reference
  • external errors, for example a comunication error with a remote DBMS or even an error inside the JVM, for example an “Out of memory error”

The OOP way of handling those problems is by handling them outside the normal control flow.

What even is an exception?

Like everything in Java an exception is an Object that is used to tell the caller method that there is a problem and it needs to be handled.

Here is what happens in detail

  • the callee method checks conditions that can create errors
  • it throws an exception if these conditions are true
  • the caller now has to handle the exception

Let’s see an example:

public float division(int a, int b){
    if (b == 0){
        throw new IllegalArgumentException("Division by zero");
    }

    return ((float) a )/ b;
}

In this case if the divisor b is zero then an exception is thrown, now the method execution stops and we return to the caller, it can do three things:

  • prevent the exception: the caller can check beforehand if b == 0, in this case the exception will not be thrown, if the caller cannot do these checks (because it would be too complex) it can do two things:
    • it can rethrow and propagate the exception to its caller because it does not have enough context or responsibility and it doesn’t know how to handle it
    • it can catch the exception with a try-catch block and handle it

You should always try to prevent the exception, because handling exceptions can be computationally expensive and since exceptions do not follow the usual control flow it is harder to program.

The try-catch block

As I said we can handle the exception by using a try-catch block, let’s see an example:

public void tryCatch(){
    try{
        int[] arr = {1, 2, 3};
        System.out.println(arr[4]);
    } 
    catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e){
        System.out.println("Caught an array index error: ");
    }
    catch (Exception e){
        System.out.println("Caught a generic exception");
    }
}

We wrap the code that could throw an exception (in this simple example an exception will surely be thrown) in the try block, if an exception is actually thrown then the execution of the try block stops and the code inside the first catch statement that matches the exception will be executed.

The order of the catch blocks matters, if we want a detailed explanation of what happened we need to put the most generic exception type at the bottom (in this case we put the base class Exception at the bottom) because otherwise the thrown exception will always be caught with the most generic error message.

Checked and unchecked exceptions

There are two big families of exceptions, checked exceptions and unchecked exceptions:

  • unchecked exceptions are tipically the result of interal errors and they are often avoidable if the programmer pays attention, also we are not obligated by the compiler to handle them
  • checked exceptions are the result of external errors and the compiler forces us to handle them (the compiler “checks” for unhandled exceptions)

We can distinguish them by looking at their superclass, checked exceptions extend Exception while unchecked exceptions extend RuntimeException.

When we throw a checked exception we need to to three things to avoid being screamed at by the compiler:

  • the exception must be declared in the called method header with the throws clause
public void close() throws IOException
  • the caller cannot ingore this exception so it either need to handle it with a try-catch block or it needs to rethrow it

(NOTE: the throws clause is compulsory for checked exceptions but it can be also used with unchecked exceptions, in this case though the compiler will not do any checks)

Gracefully restore external resources

After using external resources (connection to DBMS or access to a file) we need to restore them to a normal state even when exceptions are thrown, to do this we use the finally block.

The finally block is placed after the last catch block and it is always executed:

  • it is executed if inside the try block no exceptions are thrown
  • it is executed if inside the try block an exception is thrown and it is caught
  • it is executed if inside the try block an exception is thrown and it is uncaught

Sometimes exception can be thrown even inside a finally block, for example when we are closing a file:

finally{
    try{
        if (file != null){
            file.close()
        }
    } catch (IOException e){
        System.out.println(e.printStackTrace());
    }
}

in this case there is nothing we can do, we tried our best but we couldn’t restore the resource to a normal state.

From Java 8 onwards a new construct, try-with-resources has been added, this can be used with classes that implement AutoClosable, let’s see an example:

try (FileWriter f = new FileWriter("text.txt")){
    f.write("foo");
} catch (IOException e){
    System.out.println(e.printStackTrace());
}

when this code is run, the expression inside the parenthesis is executed before the try block, if there is an IOException the catch block will handle it otherwise we just write “foo” to the file; in any case since FileWriter implements AutoClosable, the close() method will be called and any exception thrown by it will be handled automatically without using finally.

item.title: Why does synthwave make us nostalgic?
item.description:
Why does synthwave make us nostalgic?

Before diving in our “investigation”, I wanted to tell you how this article came to life…

A few weeks ago I was cramming for my computer architecture exam and I wanted some kind of background music that was not too distracting, you might say the obvious pick would be some lo-fi hip-hop radio playlist but for me it’s way too chill and relaxing (not ideal when you are sleep deprived), so I decided to pick this synthwave playlist that I used to listen to and after a few songs an inexplicable wave of nostalgia and melanchony hit me, that’s when I started to search why is this genre so nostalgic.
item.content:
Why does synthwave make us nostalgic?

Before diving in our “investigation”, I wanted to tell you how this article came to life…

A few weeks ago I was cramming for my computer architecture exam and I wanted some kind of background music that was not too distracting, you might say the obvious pick would be some lo-fi hip-hop radio playlist but for me it’s way too chill and relaxing (not ideal when you are sleep deprived), so I decided to pick this synthwave playlist that I used to listen to and after a few songs an inexplicable wave of nostalgia and melanchony hit me, that’s when I started to search why is this genre so nostalgic.

What is synthwave?

Despite being heavily inspired by the 80s, synthwave is a relatively new genre, it was born from the minds of artists from the French electronic scene in the late 2000s, as the name suggests the genre is centered around the heavy use of analog synths and usually has a quite energetic rhythm, it is also quite similar to French house music (the only good thing France created \s) and Italo disco (the great Giorgio Moroder is one of the fathers of electronic music, which synthwave is a subgenre of)

Synthwave in pop culture

One of the things that brought synthwave to the mainstream was the movie Drive (Ryan Gosling is literally me), and its soundtrack featuring “Kavinsky”’s famous “Nightcall” and “College”’s “A Real Hero”, the film’s theme song

Another very important movie that is associated with synthwave is Blade Runner 2049, but in this case I think that people associate this movie more with the visual aesthetic of synthwave rather than the music.

The visual aesthetic

I’m sure you have all seen some pictures (like the one on header of this article) that follow the synthwave aesthetic, they all have something in common:

  • bright purple neon lights
  • futuristic cars and wirefame graphics
  • sunsets (a lot of sunsets)
  • infinite landscapes (like tall skyscraper but also mountain terrains)

And I think that the visuals also contribute to the general nostalgia feeling, for me it’s mostly the sunsets but also the infinite landscapes, they are mostly empty, there isn’t a single person and it gives off the same vibe as a liminal space.

Nostalgia for something I never lived

Let’s now try and understand where this nostalgia stems from…

As I said this music is heavily inspired from the 80s but there is a problem, I was born in the 2000s, how can I feel nostalgic about it?

The thing is I have only consumed media from the 80s sporadically and I really don’t know much about that time period so really there has to be another reason why this music makes me nostalgic…

So the only things that I think can explain this nostalgic feeling are:

  • the use of warm sounding analog synths and recording equipment, along with other effects create a kind of “past memory effect”, they make the music seem like a past memory, fuzzy and not quite clear
  • specific chord progressions that trigger this melanchony, I know that specific chords and keys can be used to bring a certain feeling to the listener but there are a couple of problems:
    • first of all there are countless synthwave songs and they all use different keys
    • then since it is electronic music, traditional chord progressions and rhythmic patterns are not always followed
    • even if a song uses particular chord progressions I have not found any specific chord that brings this sense of nostalgia, so this is a dead end

I’m going to be frank with you, I have no idea why this music brings me this melancholy and nostalgia for the good times (in my case the 2010s), and I really hope to find an answer to this someday.

In the end, synthwave is not just a genre, it’s a time machine. Whether it takes us to the 80s or to a more recent past, if we bring its glorious synth melodies and funky rhythm close to our heart it will remind us that past memories are still with us, and that the good times we miss and cherish so much are yet to come.

item.title: Conditional logic in MIPS assembly
item.description:
Conditional logic in MIPS assembly

To build traditional conditional logic the language offers us some control flow instructions and some conditional data transfer instructions, let’s see them

Mnemonic Sintax Meaning
beq (branch equal) beq $t0 $t1 Label Jumps to “Label” if $t0 and $t1 are equal
bne (branch not equal)* bne $t0 $t1 Label Jumps to “Label” if $t0 and $t1 are different
slt (set on less than)* slt $s0 $t0 $t1 Sets register $s0 to 1 if $t0 < $t1
j (jump) jump Label Jumps to Label

* there is also another variant of this instruction that compares the content of a register and an inmmediate value (slti)
item.content:
Conditional logic in MIPS assembly

To build traditional conditional logic the language offers us some control flow instructions and some conditional data transfer instructions, let’s see them

Mnemonic Sintax Meaning
beq (branch equal) beq $t0 $t1 Label Jumps to “Label” if $t0 and $t1 are equal
bne (branch not equal)* bne $t0 $t1 Label Jumps to “Label” if $t0 and $t1 are different
slt (set on less than)* slt $s0 $t0 $t1 Sets register $s0 to 1 if $t0 < $t1
j (jump) jump Label Jumps to Label

* there is also another variant of this instruction that compares the content of a register and an inmmediate value (slti)

Now we will see every condition present in any modern programming language, in each example we will translate the same if statement, but we will change the condition, here is the C code we will translate:

if (f condition g){
    f +=1;
} else {
    f -= 1;
}

We will also suppose that the variable f is the $t0 register and g is $t1

!= and == condition

To create these conditions we just need to use the provided bne, beq instructions, let’s see an example with the != condition:

beq $t0, $t1, Else
addi $t0, $t0, 1
j Exit

Else:
    subi $t0, $t0, 1

Exit:

We can see that in this example we actually use the opposite instruction (we are using beq for a != condition) but it still works, since we jump to the else label if the two variables are equal, otherwise we continue with the block inside the if statement.

>= condition

To make this condition we are going to use the slt instruction and we are going to use the same trick as before, where we negate the condition and jump to Else if the negated condition is true, let’s see:

slt $s0, $t0, $t1
bne $s0, $zero, Else
addi $t0, $t0, 1
j Exit

Else:
    subi $t0, $t0, 1

Exit:

slt $s0, $t0, $t1 is the most important line, it exploits the fact that the negated condition of < is >=, so if the condition we want to achieve is true slt sets $s0 = 0, then after that we use the trick i just told you in the previous paragraph where we jump to Else if the negated condition is true.

< condition

To obtain this condition we just need to change bne to beq in the previous snippet since now if the condition is true the output from slt will be 1, after all slt checks the < condition

slt $s0, $t0, $t1
beq $s0, $zero, Else
addi $t0, $t0, 1
j Exit

Else:
    subi $t0, $t0, 1

Exit:

> condition

To translate this condition we use the fact that by inverting the operands of a < condition we get the > condition, let’s see the implementation:

slt $s0, $t1, $t0
beq $s0, $zero, Else
addi $t0, $t0, 1
j Exit

Else:
    subi $t0, $t0, 1

Exit:

<= condition

The last condition is <=, to translate it we exploit the fact that <= is the negation of >:

slt $s0, $t1, $t0
bne $s0, $zero, Else
addi $t0, $t0, 1
j Exit

Else:
    subi $t0, $t0, 1

Exit:

We use the same check slt $s0, $t1, $t0 that we used for > but the difference is that if the condition we want to achieve is true, then slt outputs a 0 (as we said <= is the negation of >, and we just switched the operands when using slt)

And there you have it, these are all the traditional logic conditions you are familiar with, now a couple of thoughts:

  • pseudoinstructions that do these conditions (for example bgt for >) exists, these are instructions that are translated to other more basic instructions by the assembler (that’s what the $at register is used for)
  • these translations can be quite challenging to remember (and i really hope that my explanation was not too short or confusing), but recreating them could be a good exercise to learn or practice another assembly flavour (even though the ISA may give you different instructions)

If you read all of this, thank you! If you have any questions or corrections (just keep in mind English is not my mother tongue) you can put an issue on Github
